microHypotheses.statement: Keeping @convex-dev/workflow as primary orchestration will remain viable if batch submission switches from inline prompt payloads to payload-bounded chunks or reference-based loading.
microHypotheses.rationale: Current implementation already chunks batch membership and has route controls; the dominant gap is action/workflow payload size pressure from full prompt arrays.
validationCriteria: No action/workflow payload limit errors under representative peak prompt sizes
keyAssumptions: Prompt text can be reconstructed from DB refs or chunked safely before provider submit
critiquePoints: Extra DB reads for reconstruction may increase latency/cost

microHypotheses.statement: Most current contention risk can be mitigated without replacing workflow by tightening lease semantics and reducing hot-document patch frequency in batch/job polling loops.
microHypotheses.rationale: Code already contains claim leases and OCC-friendly early exits; incremental lock/patch strategy changes should reduce overlapping write windows.
validationCriteria: Lower OCC retry/failure rates in orchestrator functions
keyAssumptions: Contention is concentrated around batch/job/request status docs
critiquePoints: If contention is dominated by unrelated domain writes, lease tuning alone underdelivers

microHypotheses.statement: Workflow-heavy orchestration remains preferable to a custom scheduler-only approach because built-in retry/durability offsets complexity, provided deterministic-change risk is managed operationally.
microHypotheses.rationale: Workflow component gives durable execution and retries, while local system already depends on workflow starts per scheduler tick.
validationCriteria: Operational incidents from workflow code changes remain below baseline
No regression in recovery from transient provider failures
keyAssumptions: Deploy process can account for deterministic workflow constraints
critiquePoints: Long-lived in-flight workflows can complicate rollout cadence
